title: Random Initialization
concept: Starting weights with small random values breaks symmetry so neurons can learn different features, but randomness that is too large or too tiny can still make training unstable or weak.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the first problem random initialization is trying to solve?

options.text: The symmetry problem between identical neurons.
The choice of batch size during evaluation.

exercises.explain: Right. Before anything else, random initialization helps neurons stop behaving like clones.

cards.title: A staggered start line
cards.body: At the start of a race, runners do not all stand in the exact same footprint. They begin in slightly different positions so each lane can play its own role. Random initialization gives neurons that same tiny staggered start.

cards.title: Why random helps
cards.body: Random initialization starts each weight with a small random value instead of the exact same number. That breaks symmetry, so neurons react a little differently to the same input and begin receiving different gradients. But the scale of that randomness matters: too big can make signals blow up or saturate, while too small can make signals fade away.

cards.title: Different knobs, gentle turn
cards.body: Imagine setting up a soundboard. If every knob begins in the same place, you may keep hearing the same flat mix. Small differences help you explore. But if the knobs begin wildly high or almost completely off, the whole system sounds terrible before you even start tuning.

cards.title: Small random vs. wild random
cards.body: Suppose a hidden layer starts with small mixed positive and negative weights. One neuron may react more to vertical edges, another to brightness, another to texture. Great. But if the weights are huge, activations can become extreme immediately, making learning jerky or unstable. If the weights are microscopic, signals can become so faint that later layers barely notice them.

cards.title: Random does not mean careless
cards.body: A common myth is that random initialization is just guessing and hoping. In reality, the randomness has a job: break symmetry. Good initialization is not 'anything random' — it is controlled randomness with a sensible scale.

cards.title: Helpful randomness vs. harmful randomness
cards.body: Random starts help only when their size is reasonable.

left.text:
Break symmetry early
Signals stay usable
Neurons can specialize

right.text:
Too large: unstable or saturated signals
Too tiny: weak fading signals
Training becomes harder

exercises.prompt: What is the main reason to start hidden-layer weights with small random values?

options.text: To make neurons begin differently so they can learn different features.
To guarantee the loss is already minimized.
To remove the need for backpropagation.
To ensure every activation becomes exactly zero.

exercises.explain: Correct. Random initialization's first win is symmetry breaking: neurons stop acting like identical copies.

exercises.prompt: True or false: Once weights are random, their size no longer matters for training quality.
exercises.explain: False. Randomness alone is not enough; badly scaled weights can still make signals explode, saturate, or vanish.

exercises.sentence: Small random initialization helps break ___ between neurons.
exercises.explain: Exactly. The key first benefit is breaking symmetry so neurons can separate into different roles.

exercises.prompt: Which setup sounds healthiest for the start of training?

options.text: Weights start as small random values, so neurons differ without sending giant signals everywhere.
Weights start as enormous random values so the network learns aggressively from the first step.
Weights all start at the same zero value so every neuron stays perfectly synchronized.

exercises.explain: Yes. Healthy initialization breaks symmetry without overwhelming the network with extreme early activations.

exercises.prompt: Two teams initialize the same deep network differently. Team A uses huge random weights and sees wild, unstable activations on the first batches. Team B uses small random weights and training begins calmly. Why is Team B's choice usually better?

options.text: It still breaks symmetry, but it is less likely to create extreme signals that make learning unstable.
It prevents the network from ever needing gradients.
It forces all neurons to stay identical for fairness.
It makes the model independent of activation functions.

exercises.explain: Exactly. Good random initialization is not just 'different' — it is different by an amount that keeps early signals usable.
